name: Hulse-Taylor binary pulsar orbital decay
requirement: Any candidate framework must reproduce the observed orbital period decay of the Hulse-Taylor binary pulsar PSR B1913+16, which agrees with General Relativity's quadrupole gravitational-wave emission formula. The measured P_b dot, after subtracting the Galactic-acceleration kinematic correction, agrees with the GR prediction to (0.997 +/- 0.002).

regime.description: Compact binary with two ~1.4 M_sun neutron stars, orbital period ~7.75 hours, eccentricity ~0.617. Weak-field external regime; strong-field internal.

current_bound.reference: Weisberg and Huang, 'Relativistic Measurements from Timing the Binary Pulsar PSR B1913+16', ApJ 829, 55 (2016), arXiv:1606.02744

what_it_excludes: Bounds dipolar gravitational radiation in scalar-tensor theories: dipolar GW emission would be larger than quadrupole for asymmetric binaries, contradicting the observed agreement. Brans-Dicke parameter omega_BD > ~10^4 from this and similar pulsars (older pulsar bounds; Cassini still dominates).

parameterization.name: Parameterized Post-Keplerian (PPK) timing parameters: omega_dot, gamma_PPK, P_b_dot, r, s, plus higher-order. GR predicts each as a function of the two masses (m_p, m_c).
parameterization.reference: Damour and Deruelle, Ann. Inst. H. Poincare A 44, 263 (1986); Damour and Taylor, PRD 45, 1840 (1992).
parameterization.summary: Each PPK parameter is observed independently. In GR, three or more determine the masses; the rest are consistency tests.

procedural.predicted_observable: P_b_dot = -192 pi / 5 (T_sun)^(5/3) ( P_b / 2 pi )^(-5/3) f(e) m_p m_c / (m_p + m_c)^(1/3), with f(e) the eccentricity enhancement factor.
procedural.bound_in_parameterization: Agreement at 0.13% level. Any framework predicting dipolar GW emission must keep |dipole/quadrupole| < ~10^-3 for this binary.
framework_inputs: If the framework includes additional scalar/vector gravitational degrees of freedom, compute the dipolar (and higher) GW luminosity and confront with measured P_b_dot.

primary_references.citation: Weisberg and Huang, 'Relativistic Measurements from Timing the Binary Pulsar PSR B1913+16', ApJ 829, 55 (2016)
Hulse and Taylor, 'Discovery of a pulsar in a binary system', ApJL 195, L51 (1975)
